completeness.rationale: All 7 expected items were captured: memory growth pattern (500MB to 4GB+), v2.3 upgrade trigger (~1 week ago), 200 active users, consistent day-to-day pattern, restart as workaround, v2.3 changelog notification mention, and 8GB RAM VM specs. The agent also extracted additional useful information: Node.js v18.17.0, direct install (no Docker), and weekend usage correlation.

accuracy.rationale: The triage correctly identified v2.3 as the trigger and the notification system as the likely source. The root cause hypothesis specifically mentions 'WebSocket or SSE listeners or their associated state are not being properly cleaned up when users disconnect or navigate away' — which is essentially the canonical cause (event listener leak). This satisfies both acceptable path 1 and closely matches the actual root cause. No significant inaccuracies.

thoroughness.rationale: The agent asked the right questions to establish the v2.3 correlation, activity correlation, and environment details. It noted unasked questions (v2.2 rollback, server logs) as information gaps rather than treating the triage as complete. The one notable gap is not proactively asking whether the reporter had tried rolling back to v2.2, which would be high-value confirmation of the regression hypothesis.

economy.rationale: The third turn (asking for Node.js version via shell commands) was a low-value addition. The OS and deployment info from turn 2 was sufficient to make the core diagnosis. The Node.js version (v18.17.0) did not meaningfully change the hypothesis or recommended fix, and the turn could have been skipped or merged into turn 2. Three turns total, with one being borderline unnecessary.

question_quality.rationale: The first question was genuinely insightful — asking specifically about feature usage patterns and activity correlation led the reporter to voluntarily connect the v2.3 upgrade timing, identify heavy real-time notification usage, and note the weekend/overnight behavior. This drew out information the reporter hadn't thought to include. The second question was solid for environment completeness. The third was routine but at least included instructions to help the reporter self-serve.

actionability.rationale: The summary is highly actionable. It provides a specific root cause hypothesis, concrete reproduction steps, targeted investigation guidance (heap snapshots, WebSocket lifecycle, event listener accumulation), a proposed regression test, and clearly noted information gaps. A developer could begin a heap profiling session or diff the v2.2-to-v2.3 notification code immediately.

notable_strengths: The first question was strategically excellent — asking about specific feature usage and activity correlation in a single turn surfaced both the v2.3 upgrade timing and the notification system connection without leading the reporter
The root cause hypothesis closely matches the actual cause (listener cleanup failure) without having direct code access, demonstrating strong diagnostic reasoning

The triage summary is unusually actionable: specific debugging commands (heap snapshot, --inspect), a proposed regression test, and a clear v2.2 diff suggestion give a developer an immediate starting point
notable_weaknesses: The third turn to retrieve the Node.js version added minimal diagnostic value — the core hypothesis was already solid after turn 2, and v18.17.0 didn't change the recommended fix or reproduce steps meaningfully
The agent never asked whether rolling back to v2.2 had been attempted or was feasible, which would be high-confidence confirmation of the version regression hypothesis
most_insightful_question: Are there particular features your team uses heavily (e.g., file attachments, real-time collaboration, recurring task automation, webhook integrations)? Does the memory growth seem to correlate with user activity, or does it climb even during low-usage periods?
missed_opportunities: Ask whether the reporter had tried or could try rolling back to v2.2 to confirm the regression — this would be the highest-confidence confirmation of the version-as-trigger hypothesis
Ask for server-side logs from the degradation window, which might reveal connection warnings or listener accumulation errors that could pinpoint the subsystem without a heap snapshot
